title: Almond croissants from day-old croissants
subtitle: Soaked, filled and double-baked with almond cream
excerpt: Day-old croissants soaked in a rum-syrup, split and filled with frangipane, brushed with more frangipane on top, scattered with flaked almonds, and baked a second time. Twelve almond croissants from yesterday's leftovers.
sourceNotes: The classical French boulangerie technique of double-baking day-old croissants soaked in syrup and filled with frangipane is documented across pre-1928 French sources as a Parisian household economy. Marie-Antoine Carême and Pellaprat both note the practice as a way of reusing yesterday's bake. Modern bakery shops use the same method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake fully, cool, freeze; refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 24 if you have 24 day-old croissants.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the frangipane up to 3 days ahead and chill; the syrup up to a week ahead.

content.text: Almond croissants are an act of boulangerie economy: yesterday's plain croissants, gone slightly stale, are soaked briefly in a rum-or-vanilla syrup that softens them and adds sweetness, filled with frangipane, more frangipane spread on top, flaked almonds scattered over, and the whole thing baked again. The second bake puffs the frangipane and crisps the almonds; the croissants come out gold and softer-than-fresh.
Pair this with the laminated-croissants recipe; almond croissants are the second-day use for any leftover bake. Day-old shop-bought croissants work as well as homemade.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan and line a baking tray. Combine the {{water}} and first {{caster-sugar}} in a small pan; bring to a simmer to dissolve the sugar. Take off the heat, stir in the {{rum-dark}} and cool slightly. For the frangipane: beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and second {{caster-sugar}} until pale; beat in the {{eggs}}; fold in the {{almond-flour}} and {{almond-extract}}.

Split each day-old croissant horizontally with a serrated knife. Brush the cut surfaces generously with the warm syrup — soak rather than dab. Spread a generous tablespoon of frangipane on the bottom half; close the croissant. Spread another tablespoon of frangipane across the top of each. Scatter the {{almonds-flaked}} over. Set on the lined tray. Bake for 16–18 minutes; the frangipane should be set and deep gold, the flaked almonds should be toasted, and the croissants should be warmed through. Dust with the {{icing-sugar}} and serve warm.
